stem: A nurse is leading resuscitation for a 68-year-old male who was found unresponsive and pulseless. The code team is managing the patient according to ACLS protocol. Based on the most recent rhythm check, determine the appropriate actions to take, the potential condition, and the parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content: Situation: This is Nurse Miller leading a Code Blue in room 402. The patient is a 68-year-old male, found unresponsive and pulseless at 07:00. Background: He has a history of coronary artery disease and hypertension, admitted for syncope. Assessment: The patient is in cardiac arrest with an initial rhythm of V-Tach that degenerated into ventricular fibrillation. We have performed one defibrillation and administered epinephrine and amiodarone per ACLS. Recommendation: We are continuing high-quality CPR and require immediate direction for the next intervention.

tabs.title: Nurses' Notes
tabs.content:
07:00: Patient found unresponsive in bed. No pulse or respirations noted. Code Blue initiated. 
07:01: CPR started by nursing staff. Oxygen 100% via bag-valve-mask. 
07:02: Cardiac monitor attached, showing wide complex tachycardia. 
07:03: IV access established in right antecubital fossa with 18g catheter. 
07:04: First dose of epinephrine 1mg IV push administered. 
07:06: Rhythm check shows no change; still wide complex tachycardia. Compressions continued. 
07:08: Second dose of epinephrine 1mg IV push administered. 
07:10: Rhythm check shows ventricular fibrillation. Defibrillation attempted at 200J. 
07:12: Rhythm check shows no change; still ventricular fibrillation. Compressions continued. 
07:14: Amiodarone 300mg IV push administered. 
07:16: Rhythm check pending. Continue chest compressions.

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
TimeHeart RateBlood PressureRespiratory RateSpO2
06:0078 bpm130/80 mmHg16 breaths/min98%
06:3082 bpm128/78 mmHg18 breaths/min97%
07:000 bpm0/0 mmHg0 breaths/min0%

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
General: Unresponsive male in bed. 
Skin: Pale, cool, and diaphoretic. 
Cardiovascular: No audible heart sounds. 
Respiratory: No chest rise or fall. 
Neurological: No response to stimuli.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Code Blue Protocol: Initiate CPR, administer oxygen, establish IV access, administer medications per ACLS guidelines, prepare for defibrillation/cardioversion.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content:
Epinephrine 1mg IV push (07:04, 07:08)
Amiodarone 300mg IV push (07:14)

actions.text: Administer another dose of epinephrine 1mg IV push

hoverRationales.text: Continuous chest compressions generate minimal but critical blood flow to the brain and myocardium. Interrupting them, even for a few seconds, worsens outcomes by dropping coronary and cerebral perfusion pressure.

Although epinephrine is due, the definitive treatment for ventricular fibrillation is defibrillation. Delaying the shock to administer medications is a critical prioritization error in the ACLS algorithm.
Synchronized cardioversion delivers energy on the R-wave and is used for tachycardias with a pulse. Applying it to V-fib, which has no organized complexes, is a procedural flaw and ineffective.

Ventricular fibrillation is chaotic ventricular quivering, producing no cardiac output. Defibrillation delivers a massive, unsynchronized electrical current to depolarize the entire myocardium simultaneously, allowing the SA node to resume pacing.

Atropine is indicated for symptomatic bradycardia by blocking vagal input to the SA node. It has no effect on the chaotic electrical activity of ventricular fibrillation, making its use a pharmacological error.

The patient's airway is currently managed with a bag-valve-mask. Pausing compressions or delaying defibrillation to reassess a secured airway is a life-threatening prioritization flaw during cardiac arrest.
This is a clinical flaw in rhythm interpretation. Asystole is a non-shockable rhythm characterized by the absence of cardiac electrical activity, which is inconsistent with the documented V-fib.
This is a clinical flaw in rhythm interpretation. While also a shockable rhythm, V-Tach has organized wide complexes, whereas the notes specify V-fib, a chaotic, disorganized rhythm.

Ventricular fibrillation is a lethal arrhythmia characterized by chaotic, uncoordinated ventricular contractions, resulting in no cardiac output. This aligns with the patient's pulseless state and the documented rhythm.

This is a clinical flaw in rhythm interpretation. PEA is a non-shockable rhythm with organized electrical activity but no pulse. The patient's documented rhythm is disorganized ventricular fibrillation.
This is a clinical flaw in monitoring. Pulse oximetry requires pulsatile arterial flow to function, which is absent in cardiac arrest. Relying on this data is misleading and incorrect.

End-tidal CO2 directly reflects pulmonary blood flow, which is generated by chest compressions. Monitoring EtCO2 provides immediate feedback on compression quality and is the earliest indicator of ROSC.

This is a clinical flaw in monitoring. Blood pressure is a measure of cardiac output and systemic resistance, both of which are effectively zero in cardiac arrest. This parameter is unmeasurable until ROSC.

This is a clinical flaw in assessment timing. The patient is unresponsive by definition of cardiac arrest. Neurological assessment is only performed after Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC) is achieved.

The cardiac rhythm dictates the entire ACLS algorithm for pulseless arrest. Continuous monitoring is required to identify the rhythm, determine if it is shockable, and evaluate the response to defibrillation and medications.
This is a clinical flaw in monitoring focus. Urine output is a marker of long-term organ perfusion and is irrelevant during the acute minutes of a cardiac arrest resuscitation.
